Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.front: ⚠️ TRAP SCENARIO — The Judicial Committee of the Privy Council, composed entirely of current UK Supreme Court justices, decides a case on employment law from the Cayman Islands. The decision directly addresses a point of English employment law that the UK Supreme Court has not yet ruled on. The Court of Appeal is later asked to follow the JCPC decision. A barrister argues the CA must follow the JCPC ruling because it consists of the same justices as the Supreme Court. Is this correct?
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.answer:
No. The barrister's argument is wrong. The JCPC decision is not binding on the Court of Appeal.

The Judicial Committee of the Privy Council is not part of the English court hierarchy. Its decisions are persuasive only — never binding on any English court, including the Court of Appeal, regardless of who sits on the Board.

⚠️ The trap: the composition of the JCPC (current SC justices) is irrelevant to its formal status in the English precedent hierarchy. The institutional position of the court — not the identity of its judges — determines whether decisions are binding. The Privy Council hears appeals from Commonwealth jurisdictions, not from English courts. It is not an appellate court in the English hierarchy.

However, JCPC decisions carry very significant persuasive weight — especially where the Board consists of current SC justices deciding a point of English common law that the SC has not addressed. The CA is likely to follow the JCPC decision in practice, but it is not obliged to do so.

The CA retains its own discretion to decline to follow the JCPC ruling if it sees good reason, though this would be unusual where the Board was composed of SC justices. The CA cannot use a JCPC decision as a freestanding basis to avoid its own binding precedents under the Young v Bristol Aeroplane framework.
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.summary_line: ⚠️ JCPC NEVER BINDING · COMPOSITION IRRELEVANT · PERSUASIVE ONLY · NOT IN ENGLISH HIERARCHY · CA HAS DISCRETION · HIGH PERSUASIVE WEIGHT IN PRACTICE · BARRISTER WRONG

Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.front: ⚠️ TRAP SCENARIO — The Court of Appeal (Civil Division) is considering a negligence case. A barrister draws attention to an ECtHR Grand Chamber decision handed down last month that directly addresses the same legal issue and reaches a conclusion inconsistent with an existing binding CA decision. She argues: (a) the ECtHR decision binds the CA under s.2 HRA; and (b) alternatively, the ECtHR decision is a Supreme Court-level inconsistent decision under the second Young v Bristol exception. Assess both arguments.
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.answer:
Both arguments fail, though for different reasons.

Argument (a) — ECtHR decision binds the CA under s.2 HRA:
Section 2 HRA requires UK courts to 'take into account' ECtHR decisions — not to follow or be bound by them. Strasbourg jurisprudence is persuasive, never binding. R v Horncastle [2009] confirmed that the UK Supreme Court itself declined to follow a clear Grand Chamber ruling where there were cogent domestic reasons not to do so. A fortiori, the CA is not bound. Argument (a) fails.

Argument (b) — ECtHR as a 'Supreme Court-level' inconsistency under Young v Bristol exception 2:
The second Young v Bristol exception applies where a prior CA decision cannot stand with a subsequent decision of the House of Lords/Supreme Court. This exception applies only to decisions of the UK Supreme Court (or its predecessor, the House of Lords) — it does not extend to decisions of international courts, including the ECtHR. The ECtHR is not part of the English court hierarchy. Argument (b) fails.

⚠️ The trap: assuming Strasbourg authority operates like SC authority in the precedent hierarchy. It does not. ECtHR decisions are persuasive aids to interpretation under s.2 — they do not rank in the court hierarchy or trigger Young v Bristol exceptions.

The barrister's best approach: argue the ECtHR decision is highly persuasive, that following the existing CA decision would put the UK in breach of its Convention obligations, and invite the CA to depart per incuriam if the CA decision ignored relevant HRA authority — or to apply s.3 interpretively.
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.summary_line: ⚠️ ECtHR NOT BINDING · S.2 = TAKE INTO ACCOUNT · HORNCASTLE · NOT 'SC-LEVEL' FOR YOUNG v BRISTOL · ECtHR NOT IN ENGLISH HIERARCHY · PERSUASIVE ONLY · BOTH ARGUMENTS FAIL

Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

The Judicial Committee of the Privy Council, sitting as a Board of seven, hands down a judgment in an employment law case from Bermuda. The Board is composed entirely of current UK Supreme Court justices, including the President of the Supreme Court. The judgment directly addresses a point of English employment law on which the Supreme Court has not yet ruled, and four of the seven justices write separately to confirm that the same reasoning applies to English law.

The following week, the Court of Appeal is asked to follow the JCPC decision in an English employment case raising the identical point. The appellant's barrister argues: 'This is effectively a Supreme Court decision. Seven Supreme Court justices have decided this point. The Court of Appeal is bound.'
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.issue: The trap is the composition of the Board. Seven current Supreme Court justices — including the President — is about as authoritative as a court can get in terms of the identity of its members. Four of them expressly confirm the reasoning applies to English law. The instinctive conclusion is that this is functionally a Supreme Court decision and the Court of Appeal must follow it. This is wrong.
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.rule: The Judicial Committee of the Privy Council is not part of the English court hierarchy. Its decisions are persuasive only — never binding on any English court, including the Court of Appeal. The institutional position of the court in the hierarchy, not the identity of the judges sitting on it, determines whether decisions are binding. The JCPC hears appeals from Commonwealth jurisdictions and the Crown Dependencies; it is not an appellate court in the English system. No matter how many Supreme Court justices sit, a JCPC decision cannot bind the Court of Appeal.
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.application: The red herring is the composition and the express statements by four justices about English law. Both features dramatically increase the persuasive weight of the decision — a Court of Appeal would need a very strong reason to depart from it — but they do not change its formal status. The barrister's argument confuses persuasive authority with binding authority. The Court of Appeal is not bound. In practice, it will almost certainly follow the JCPC decision given the composition, but that is a choice, not a compulsion.
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.conclusion: JCPC = PERSUASIVE ONLY REGARDLESS OF COMPOSITION. Institutional position in the hierarchy determines binding status, not the identity of judges. The Court of Appeal is not bound. The trap: SC justices sitting on JCPC = assumed to be SC-equivalent authority.
Binding vs Persuasive Precedent.summary_line: JCPC NOT IN ENGLISH HIERARCHY · COMPOSITION IRRELEVANT · PERSUASIVE ONLY · CA NOT BOUND · ⚠️ TRAP: SC JUSTICES ON JCPC = ASSUMED BINDING

Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.subtopic: Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling, Reversing, Per Incuriam
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.front: What is the precise distinction between overruling and reversing a judicial decision? Why does the distinction matter?
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.answer:
These are commonly confused but technically distinct:

Overruling: A superior court declares that the legal principle (ratio) stated in an earlier decision was wrong. The earlier case ceases to be good law for future cases. The parties to the original case are not affected — overruling only changes the legal principle going forward.

Reversing: A court on appeal sets aside the decision of the lower court in the same case. The parties' rights are directly affected — the losing party in the lower court wins on appeal. Reversing operates within the same litigation.

Example: The Supreme Court, in Case X, states that the Court of Appeal's principle in Case Y (decided 10 years ago) was wrong — that is overruling. The Supreme Court, hearing Case X on appeal from the Court of Appeal's decision in Case X, says the Court of Appeal was wrong and rules for the appellant — that is reversing.

⚠️ This is a very high-frequency SQE exam trap. Students regularly confuse these. The key: Overruling = wrong principle declared in a different/prior case. Reversing = same case, higher court on appeal. A scenario describing a superior court finding an earlier case was wrongly decided is overruling. A scenario describing a higher court allowing an appeal in the same proceedings is reversing.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.summary_line: OVERRULING v REVERSING · OVERRULING = WRONG PRINCIPLE IN PRIOR CASE · REVERSING = SAME CASE ON APPEAL · PARTIES ONLY AFFECTED BY REVERSAL · ⚠️ KEY EXAM TRAP

Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.subtopic: Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling, Reversing, Per Incuriam
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.front: What is distinguishing in the context of judicial precedent, and when may a court use it?
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.answer:
Distinguishing is the technique by which a court declines to follow an otherwise binding precedent by identifying a material difference between the facts of the earlier case and the case before it. If the facts are materially different, the ratio of the precedent does not apply and the court is not bound.

A court may distinguish where:
(i) There is a genuine factual distinction between the two cases; and
(ii) That distinction is material — i.e. had the earlier court been faced with the same (different) facts, it might have decided differently.

Distinguishing allows the law to develop and adapt to new factual situations without requiring a superior court to overrule.

⚠️

**Limits:** Distinguishing must be based on a genuine and material factual distinction, not an artificial or pretextual one. Courts that distinguish on spurious grounds are open to criticism. A court that finds a material factual difference loses the binding force of the earlier precedent but does not overrule it — the earlier case remains good law for cases with those facts.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.summary_line: DISTINGUISHING · MATERIAL FACTUAL DIFFERENCE · COURT NOT BOUND · GENUINE DISTINCTION REQUIRED · PRIOR CASE STILL GOOD LAW · LAW DEVELOPS INCREMENTALLY

Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.subtopic: Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling, Reversing, Per Incuriam
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.front: What is the per incuriam doctrine and when does it apply?
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.answer:
A decision is per incuriam ('through lack of care') where it was made in ignorance of, or in breach of, a binding statutory provision or authoritative case authority that would have materially affected the outcome. A per incuriam decision is not binding and may be departed from.

The doctrine was confirmed in Young v Bristol Aeroplane Co [1944] as one of the three exceptions allowing the Court of Appeal to depart from its own previous decisions.

Requirements (applied strictly):

1. The case or statute must have been relevant and binding;

2. It must not have been cited to the court;

3. It must have been capable of materially affecting the outcome.

⚠️ Per incuriam is applied very narrowly. A decision is not per incuriam simply because:
— The judgment is poorly reasoned
— An argument was not put before the court (as opposed to a binding authority not cited)
— The court reached a debatable conclusion
The doctrine requires a clear and identifiable gap in the court's awareness of binding authority.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.summary_line: PER INCURIAM · LACK OF CARE · IGNORED BINDING AUTHORITY OR STATUTE · NOT BINDING · YOUNG v BRISTOL AEROPLANE · THREE REQUIREMENTS · APPLIED NARROWLY

Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.subtopic: Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling, Reversing, Per Incuriam
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.front: ⚠️ TRAP SCENARIO — The Court of Appeal in Case A (2018) established that online platform operators are vicariously liable for the acts of independent contractors in certain circumstances. In Case B (2025), the Supreme Court hears Case A on appeal and holds that the Court of Appeal was wrong — platform operators are not vicariously liable for independent contractors in those circumstances. A solicitor advises her client: 'The Supreme Court has reversed the Court of Appeal's decision in Case A.' Is the solicitor's advice accurate?
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.answer:
No. The solicitor's advice is inaccurate. This is a reversal not because of incorrect terminology — actually, it IS a reversal — wait, let me think carefully.

Actually: the Supreme Court is hearing Case A on appeal. It hears Case A and holds the Court of Appeal was wrong. This IS the same case — Case A — being decided by the Supreme Court. This is reversing, not overruling.

Reversal: a court on appeal sets aside the decision of a lower court in the same case. This is what has happened — the SC heard Case A (the same litigation) on appeal from the CA and reached the opposite conclusion.

The solicitor said 'reversed' — which is actually correct here. The SC has reversed the CA decision in Case A.

⚠️ However, there is an important secondary question: has the SC overruled the legal principle from Case A for future cases? Yes — by reversing the CA's decision, the SC has also established (in its own ratio) that platform operators are not vicariously liable for independent contractors. This ratio now overrules the CA's principle for future cases.

So the terminology the solicitor used — 'reversed' — is accurate for describing what happened to Case A itself. What may be imprecise is if the solicitor stops there: the SC's decision also overrules the CA's principle (establishes a new binding precedent) for all future cases.

Precise advice: 'The SC reversed the CA decision in Case A (the same litigation) and in doing so overruled the CA's vicarious liability principle for future cases.'
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.summary_line: REVERSING v OVERRULING · SAME CASE ON APPEAL = REVERSAL · SC DECISION ALSO OVERRULES PRINCIPLE FOR FUTURE · BOTH CONCEPTS APPLY · PRECISE TERMINOLOGY MATTERS

Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.subtopic: Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling, Reversing, Per Incuriam
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.front: ⚠️ TRAP SCENARIO — The Court of Appeal decided in Fernley v Argent Ltd [2019] that employers are not liable in negligence for psychiatric harm caused to employees by excessive workloads unless the employer had actual knowledge of a specific risk to that employee. In 2025, a High Court judge is hearing a similar case. She finds the Fernley principle is wrong and unjust. She considers departing from it. Can she?
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.answer:
No. The High Court judge cannot depart from Fernley v Argent Ltd [2019] simply because she considers it wrong and unjust.

The doctrine of stare decisis requires that a lower court follow the ratio decidendi of a superior court. The Court of Appeal is superior to the High Court in the hierarchy. Fernley v Argent Ltd is a binding Court of Appeal decision, and its ratio on employer liability for psychiatric harm binds the High Court judge.

The judge's personal view that the principle is wrong and unjust is irrelevant to the question of whether she is bound. Only the Supreme Court — using the Practice Statement 1966 — or Parliament can change this rule. The High Court has no equivalent discretion.

What the judge can do:

1. Apply Fernley but note her disagreement clearly, inviting the parties to appeal so the CA or SC can reconsider.

2. Distinguish on the facts — if the facts of the present case are materially different from Fernley (e.g. the employer here did have actual knowledge), she need not follow the ratio.

3. Find Fernley was decided per incuriam — if it ignored a binding statute or authority — in which case she need not follow it.

⚠️ The trap: courts below the CA cannot simply choose not to follow binding CA precedent because they disagree with it. The only escape routes are distinguishing on the facts, per incuriam, or appealing upward.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.summary_line: HIGH COURT BOUND BY CA · STARE DECISIS · CANNOT DEPART BECAUSE WRONG/UNJUST · DISTINGUISH OR APPEAL · PER INCURIAM POSSIBLE · ⚠️ TRAP: LOWER COURTS CANNOT CHOOSE TO IGNORE BINDING PRECEDENT

Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.subtopic: Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling, Reversing, Per Incuriam
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.front: In Haslam v Greenwood Hotels [2021] CA, the Court of Appeal held that a hotel owes a duty of care to guests injured by a latent defect in their room if the hotel had been notified of similar defects in other rooms in the past six months. In a new High Court case, the claimant was injured by a latent defect in her room. The hotel was not notified of any similar defects in other rooms, but it had received a complaint about a defect in the same room six weeks earlier. Can the High Court distinguish Haslam?
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.answer:
Yes — there is a strong basis for distinguishing Haslam v Greenwood Hotels [2021].

The ratio of Haslam, on these facts, is that notification of similar defects in other rooms in the past six months triggers the duty of care. The factual trigger in Haslam is:

1. similar defects,
2. in other rooms,
3. within six months.

The new case has different material facts:
— The hotel was not notified of similar defects in other rooms — fact
2. is absent.
— Instead, the hotel had a complaint about a defect in the same room six weeks earlier — a factually distinct notification.

Grounds for distinguishing: The CA's ratio in Haslam was built around the specific factual pattern of cross-room notification. The case before the High Court involves a prior complaint about the same room — arguably a stronger basis for a duty of care (the hotel knew of a problem in that specific room) but factually different from the Haslam scenario.

Effect of distinguishing: The High Court is not bound by Haslam on these different facts. It may reach its own conclusion on whether a duty arises where the hotel had prior notice of a problem in the same room specifically. It might well find a duty exists on an even stronger basis — but it does so as an independent decision, not following Haslam.

⚠️ Distinguishing must be based on a genuine and material factual difference — which it is here. The distinction is not pretextual.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.summary_line: DISTINGUISHING · MATERIAL FACTUAL DIFFERENCE · HASLAM RATIO = OTHER ROOMS NOTIFIED · NEW CASE = SAME ROOM NOTIFIED · GENUINE DISTINCTION · HC NOT BOUND · MAY FIND STRONGER DUTY

Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.subtopic: Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling, Reversing, Per Incuriam
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

The Court of Appeal decided in Hartley v Meridian Bank plc [2019] that banks owe a duty of care to guarantors when providing information about the financial position of the principal debtor. Meridian Bank appeals to the Supreme Court.

In 2024, the Supreme Court hears the appeal in Hartley v Meridian Bank plc and holds that the Court of Appeal was wrong — no such duty of care exists. The Supreme Court allows the bank's appeal.

A law student preparing for an exam reads the Supreme Court judgment and writes in her notes: 'The SC overruled the Court of Appeal in Hartley v Meridian Bank. Overruling is when a higher court disapproves of a lower court's decision in the same case.'
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.issue: The trap is subtle and targets the precise distinction between reversing and overruling — two concepts that look similar because both involve a higher court disagreeing with a lower court's legal analysis. The student has correctly identified that the SC found the CA was wrong. She has incorrectly labelled the mechanism and — crucially — incorrectly defined 'overruling'. The error is definitional but has real analytical consequences.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.rule: Reversing: a court on appeal sets aside the decision of a lower court in the same litigation. The parties are the same, the case is the same, and the higher court substitutes its decision for the lower court's. Overruling: a court in a later, different case holds that a previous decision in an earlier, different case was wrongly decided. The overruled decision ceases to be good law as a precedent for future cases, but the parties in the original case are unaffected by the overruling. In Hartley v Meridian Bank, the SC is hearing Hartley on appeal — the same litigation. The SC has reversed the CA's decision. It has not overruled it.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.application: The red herring is that the SC found the CA was wrong. Both reversing and overruling involve a higher court finding a lower court's reasoning incorrect — the distinction lies in whether it is the same case or a different case. The student's definition ('overruling is when a higher court disapproves of a lower court's decision in the same case') is the definition of reversing. What actually happened: the SC reversed the CA in Hartley. The SC's decision in Hartley also establishes the correct legal position (no duty of care to guarantors) — meaning that any future case relying on the Hartley CA decision as precedent would find it has been overruled. But the mechanism that happened in Hartley itself is reversal, not overruling.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.conclusion: SAME CASE ON APPEAL = REVERSAL. DIFFERENT CASE, LATER DATE = OVERRULING. The SC reversed the CA in Hartley. The student's definition is wrong. The trap: conflating 'higher court finds lower court wrong' with 'overruling' regardless of whether it is the same or a different case.
Techniques: Distinguishing, Overruling,.summary_line: REVERSING = SAME CASE ON APPEAL · OVERRULING = DIFFERENT LATER CASE · SC REVERSED CA IN HARTLEY · STUDENT'S DEFINITION WRONG · ⚠️ TRAP: 'HIGHER COURT FINDS WRONG' = ASSUMED OVERRULING

The Practice Statement 1966.front: What did the Practice Statement [1966] 1 WLR 1234 establish and why was it significant?
The Practice Statement 1966.answer:
The Practice Statement, issued by Lord Gardiner LC on behalf of the House of Lords in 1966, provided that while the Lords treated their own past decisions as normally binding, they would depart from a previous decision 'when it appears right to do so'.

Significance: Before 1966, the House of Lords was absolutely bound by its own previous decisions: London Street Tramways v London County Council [1898]. The Practice Statement overturned this, giving the Lords (now the Supreme Court) flexibility to correct past errors without waiting for Parliament to legislate.

Factors considered when deciding whether to depart:
— Whether the earlier decision has worked injustice or inconvenience
— Whether legal development has overtaken the earlier rule
— Whether departing would upset settled transactions or alter previously acquired rights

The Practice Statement was confirmed to apply to the Supreme Court: R (Knauer) v Ministry of Justice [2016].

⚠️ Link — Court of Appeal contrasted (see FLK1-LSYS-040): The CA has no equivalent of the Practice Statement — it is strictly bound by its own decisions under Young v Bristol Aeroplane [1944] save for three exhaustive exceptions. This is a critical exam distinction.
The Practice Statement 1966.summary_line: PRACTICE STATEMENT 1966 · HOUSE OF LORDS · DEPART WHEN RIGHT TO DO SO · LONDON STREET TRAMWAYS OVERTURNED · R (KNAUER) v MOJ · APPLIES TO SUPREME COURT

The Practice Statement 1966.front: Why have the courts been reluctant to use the Practice Statement 1966 frequently, and name two occasions where it has been used.
The Practice Statement 1966.answer:
Reasons for reluctance:

1. Legal certainty and predictability: Overruling established precedent undermines parties' ability to rely on settled law.

2. Retrospective effect: Overruling changes the law for past transactions and completed events — potentially unsettling acquired rights.

3. Parliamentary role: If the law is unsatisfactory, the proper remedy may be legislation — especially on policy-laden questions.

4. Finality: As the final court, there is no appeal if the Practice Statement produces the wrong result.

Two notable uses:

1. British Railways Board v Herrington [1972] — overruled Robert Addie & Sons (Collieries) v Dumbreck [1929] on the duty of care owed to child trespassers, replacing the minimal common law standard with a duty of 'common humanity'.

2. R v R [1991] — overruled the common law marital rape immunity, holding that a husband could be convicted of raping his wife.

⚠️ Also note: Murphy v Brentwood DC [1990] (overruled Anns v Merton on defective buildings); Pepper v Hart [1992] (overruled the common law exclusionary rule on Hansard use).
The Practice Statement 1966.summary_line: PRACTICE STATEMENT RARELY USED · CERTAINTY · RETROSPECTIVE EFFECT · PARLIAMENTARY ROLE · FINALITY · BRB v HERRINGTON · R v R · MURPHY v BRENTWOOD · PEPPER v HART

The Practice Statement 1966.front: London Street Tramways Co v London County Council [1898] AC 375
The Practice Statement 1966.issue: Whether the House of Lords was absolutely bound by its own previous decisions, or whether it retained a discretion to depart from them in appropriate cases.
The Practice Statement 1966.rule: The House of Lords is absolutely bound by its own previous decisions. Once the Lords have decided a point of law, that decision is final and conclusive, and subsequent Lords must follow it even if they consider it wrong. The only remedy for an erroneous House of Lords decision is legislation by Parliament.
The Practice Statement 1966.application: The House of Lords affirmed the principle of absolute self-binding. Lord Halsbury LC reasoned that finality and certainty in the law — so that parties could order their affairs and transactions on the basis of known legal rules — outweighed the disadvantage of occasionally perpetuating an error. It was better to have certainty even at the cost of correctness.
The Practice Statement 1966.conclusion: The absolute self-binding rule was established as the governing constitutional position for the House of Lords. This remained the rule until the Practice Statement [1966] 1 WLR 1234, in which Lord Gardiner LC announced on behalf of the Lords that they would 'depart from a previous decision when it appears right to do so'. London Street Tramways was thereby overruled as a constitutional matter — and the Practice Statement confirmed to apply to the Supreme Court in R (Knauer) v Ministry of Justice [2016].
The Practice Statement 1966.summary_line: HOUSE OF LORDS ABSOLUTELY BOUND · FINALITY OVER CORRECTNESS · CERTAINTY RATIONALE · OVERRULED BY PRACTICE STATEMENT 1966 · LORD HALSBURY · PRE-1966 POSITION

The Practice Statement 1966.front: British Railways Board v Herrington [1972] AC 877
The Practice Statement 1966.issue: Whether the House of Lords should use the Practice Statement 1966 to depart from Robert Addie & Sons (Collieries) Ltd v Dumbreck [1929], which had held that an occupier's only duty to a child trespasser was to refrain from deliberately or recklessly injuring them.
The Practice Statement 1966.rule: Under the Practice Statement 1966, the House of Lords departed from Addie v Dumbreck and established a new test: an occupier owes a duty of 'common humanity' to child trespassers. The duty takes into account the degree of danger, the likelihood of children trespassing, the occupier's resources, and what a humane person would do in the circumstances.
The Practice Statement 1966.application: A six-year-old boy was severely injured when he crawled through a gap in a fence onto the railway line and touched live rails. BRB knew of the gap and that children regularly passed through. The HL held that the Addie v Dumbreck rule — which owed nothing to children who trespassed — was outdated, harsh, and inconsistent with modern standards of humanity. Using the Practice Statement for the first time in this factual context, the Lords overruled Addie and held that BRB had breached its duty of common humanity.
The Practice Statement 1966.conclusion: This was one of the first significant uses of the Practice Statement 1966. It demonstrated that the Lords would use the power to correct precedents that had produced unjust outcomes over time, particularly where social conditions had changed and the original reasoning no longer reflected modern expectations. The 'common humanity' duty was later replaced by the Occupiers' Liability Act 1984, which now governs the duty to trespassers.
The Practice Statement 1966.summary_line: PRACTICE STATEMENT 1966 · FIRST MAJOR USE · ADDIE v DUMBRECK OVERRULED · CHILD TRESPASSERS · DUTY OF COMMON HUMANITY · SOCIAL CHANGE JUSTIFICATION · LATER REPLACED BY OLA 1984

The Practice Statement 1966.issue: Whether a husband could be convicted of raping his wife, having regard to the common law rule attributed to Sir Matthew Hale (1736) that a wife gave irrevocable consent to sexual intercourse with her husband upon marriage.
The Practice Statement 1966.rule: Using the Practice Statement 1966, the House of Lords overruled the marital rape immunity. A husband can be convicted of raping his wife. The common law fiction that marriage amounts to irrevocable consent to sexual intercourse was anachronistic and had no place in modern law. Marriage does not deprive a wife of her right to refuse sexual intercourse.
The Practice Statement 1966.application: The defendant separated from his wife, then broke into her parents' house and raped her. He argued the marital rape immunity applied. The trial judge rejected this argument, he was convicted, and the Court of Appeal (which had also departed from the old rule) and House of Lords upheld the conviction. The HL confirmed that the common law evolves and courts have the power to remove anachronistic rules that are contrary to fundamental principles of justice.
The Practice Statement 1966.conclusion:
R v R is one of the most significant uses of the Practice Statement 1966. It confirms that the Practice Statement can be used to remove an established but unjust common law rule, and that even a rule of long standing is not immune from overruling if it has become inconsistent with modern legal values.

⚠️ The ECtHR subsequently confirmed in SW v United Kingdom [1995] that the conviction did not violate Article 7 ECHR (no retrospective criminal law) because the evolution of the common law was foreseeable.
The Practice Statement 1966.summary_line: PRACTICE STATEMENT 1966 · MARITAL RAPE IMMUNITY ABOLISHED · HALE'S RULE OVERRULED · ANACHRONISTIC RULE · EVOLVING COMMON LAW · ECtHR: SW v UK [1995] ART.7 SATISFIED

The Practice Statement 1966.front: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398
The Practice Statement 1966.issue: Whether a local authority owed a duty of care in negligence to the owner of a house where the authority had approved defective foundations, causing the house to be at risk of structural failure, and whether Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728 should be overruled.
The Practice Statement 1966.rule: Using the Practice Statement 1966, the House of Lords overruled Anns v Merton LBC and held that a local authority does not owe a duty of care in negligence to a building owner in respect of pure economic loss caused by defective foundations. Liability in negligence requires physical injury or damage to property other than the defective item itself; pure economic loss (diminution in value of the property) is not recoverable.
The Practice Statement 1966.application: Murphy bought a house whose foundations, approved by the council, were defective. When the defect became apparent the house was unsaleable except at a loss. He claimed the council owed him a duty of care in approving the foundations. The HL, departing from Anns (which had held a duty existed), drew a clear line: the defective property is not 'damage' to other property — it is simply defective. Pure economic loss from building defects is not recoverable in negligence from local authorities.
The Practice Statement 1966.conclusion: Anns v Merton was overruled. Murphy v Brentwood established the 'no liability for pure economic loss' principle in this context and remains good law. The case illustrates the Practice Statement being used to correct a precedent considered to have unduly extended tortious liability. It also confirmed that courts will use the Practice Statement to undo expansions of liability that have proved unworkable or unprincipled.
The Practice Statement 1966.summary_line: PRACTICE STATEMENT 1966 · ANNS v MERTON OVERRULED · NO DUTY FOR PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · DEFECTIVE BUILDING · LOCAL AUTHORITY NEGLIGENCE · MURPHY v BRENTWOOD [1991]

The Practice Statement 1966.front: The Supreme Court is considering whether to use the Practice Statement 1966 to depart from a 1972 House of Lords decision that has been applied consistently for over 50 years. Counsel argues: 'The 1972 decision was wrong and this Court should correct it.' What factors will the Supreme Court weigh, and is the Practice Statement likely to be used?
The Practice Statement 1966.answer:
The SC will weigh the following factors before deciding whether to use the Practice Statement:

Factors favouring departure:
— The 1972 decision was wrong — a clear legal error is the primary driver for using the Practice Statement. The SC should not perpetuate incorrect law.
— If the error has caused ongoing injustice or practical inconvenience in the intervening 50 years, this strengthens the case for correction.
— If legal developments since 1972 (legislation, other case law) have overtaken the original reasoning, departure becomes more justified.

Factors against departure:
— Legal certainty and reliance: parties have ordered their affairs on the basis of the 1972 rule for 50 years. Overruling creates retrospective uncertainty — people may have structured transactions, drafted contracts, or planned their lives around the existing rule.
— Finality: as the final court, if the Practice Statement produces the wrong result, there is no further appeal.
— Parliamentary role: if the rule produces unjust results, Parliament has had 50 years to legislate — it may be better for change to come from the legislature, especially on a policy-laden question.
— Risk of unintended consequences: overruling a long-established rule after 50 years may have wide-ranging effects on related areas of law that have developed around it.

Likely outcome: the SC is cautious about using the Practice Statement (it is rarely invoked). 50 years of consistent application is a strong reason for caution. The SC is more likely to use it where the error is clear, the injustice obvious, and disruption to settled law manageable. Counsel must address the reliance/certainty point directly.
The Practice Statement 1966.summary_line: PRACTICE STATEMENT 1966 · FACTORS FOR · LEGAL ERROR · INJUSTICE · LEGAL DEVELOPMENT · FACTORS AGAINST · CERTAINTY/RELIANCE · FINALITY · PARLIAMENTARY ROLE · RARELY INVOKED

The Practice Statement 1966.front: Going deeper: Is the Supreme Court too cautious in using the Practice Statement 1966, and what does this reveal about the relationship between judicial lawmaking and democratic legitimacy?
The Practice Statement 1966.answer:
The Practice Statement 1966 announced that the Lords would 'depart from a previous decision when it appears right to do so'. In practice, the Lords and then the Supreme Court have used it sparingly — perhaps 20-30 times in 60 years. The question is whether this restraint is constitutionally appropriate or reflects judicial timidity.

The case for caution (Cross, 'Precedent in English Law' [1991]; Stevens, 'The English Judges' [2002]): Legal certainty and the reliance interests of parties who have structured transactions, made decisions, and organised their affairs on the basis of established rules are genuine and weighty. The SC sits at the apex of the hierarchy — its decisions affect enormous numbers of people and transactions. Getting overruling wrong is a serious institutional mistake. And where a precedent produces injustice, Parliament can act — it is not the SC's job to substitute for legislation.

The case for greater willingness (Duxbury, 'The Nature and Authority of Precedent' [2008]): The rarity of Practice Statement use means that demonstrably wrong decisions persist for years or decades. London Street Tramways established absolute HL self-binding for 68 years. Murphy v Brentwood took until 1991 to remove the Anns expansion. If courts are too deferential to their own errors, the law stagnates and litigants bear the cost. The SC's use of the Practice Statement in R v Knauer [2016] (to correct a 1981 damages miscalculation) took 35 years.

The democratic legitimacy question: Overruling a precedent is a form of judicial lawmaking — retrospective in effect. When the SC overrules a 50-year-old decision, it changes the law that applied to all the transactions entered into during those 50 years. This is constitutionally awkward in a democracy: the SC is making law without a parliamentary mandate, often affecting far more people than the parties before it. Critics (Griffith, 'The Politics of the Judiciary' [1997]) argue this reveals that senior judges are policymakers — and unaccountable ones.

The comparative perspective: The US Supreme Court has a broader record of overruling its own precedents (Obergefell v Hodges [2015]; Dobbs v Jackson [2022]). Critics of US constitutional law argue that instability in overruling produces uncertainty and undermines the rule of law. The UK's more cautious approach may reflect a wiser institutional instinct — but the price is slower correction of error.

Unresolved question: There is no agreed metric for when the Practice Statement should be used. The 'appears right to do so' formulation gives no guidance on how to weigh certainty against correctness, or how long an error must persist before overruling is justified. This is a genuine gap in the theoretical account of the doctrine.
The Practice Statement 1966.summary_line: PRACTICE STATEMENT 1966 · JUDICIAL RESTRAINT · CROSS · STEVENS · DUXBURY · DEMOCRATIC LEGITIMACY · GRIFFITH · RETROSPECTIVE LAWMAKING · COMPARATIVE (US) · NO AGREED METRIC · UNRESOLVED

Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.subtopic: Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aeroplane
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.front: What is the general rule from Young v Bristol Aeroplane Co [1944] regarding the Court of Appeal being bound by its own decisions?
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.answer:
The general rule established in Young v Bristol Aeroplane Co [1944] is that the Court of Appeal (Civil Division) is bound by its own previous decisions, subject to three specified exceptions.

Rationale: The Court of Appeal hears a very high volume of appeals. Consistency and predictability require that it follows its own earlier decisions. Without this rule, the outcome of an appeal could depend on which panel of Lord Justices happened to hear the case.

Unlike the Supreme Court, the Court of Appeal has no equivalent of the Practice Statement 1966 — it cannot depart from its own decisions simply because it considers them wrong. The three exceptions in Young v Bristol Aeroplane are exhaustive for the Civil Division.

⚠️ Link — Supreme Court contrasted (see FLK1-LSYS-038): Unlike the CA, the Supreme Court may depart from its own previous decisions under the Practice Statement 1966 whenever it appears right to do so — a significantly broader discretion. Applying the Practice Statement test to the CA, or applying the Young v Bristol exceptions to the SC, are both common exam errors.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.summary_line: CA BOUND BY OWN DECISIONS · YOUNG v BRISTOL AEROPLANE [1944] · CIVIL DIVISION · HIGH VOLUME RATIONALE · NO PRACTICE STATEMENT EQUIVALENT · THREE EXCEPTIONS ONLY · ⚠️ CONTRAST WITH SC

Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.subtopic: Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aeroplane
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.front: State precisely the three exceptions in Young v Bristol Aeroplane Co [1944] that allow the Court of Appeal to depart from its own previous decisions.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.answer:
In Young v Bristol Aeroplane Co [1944], the Court of Appeal identified three exceptions to the general rule that it is bound by its own previous decisions:

1. Conflicting decisions: Where there are two conflicting Court of Appeal decisions, the later court must choose which to follow — the other is treated as implicitly overruled.

2. Supreme Court inconsistency: Where a Court of Appeal decision, although not expressly overruled, cannot stand with a subsequent decision of the House of Lords (now the Supreme Court) — the Court of Appeal must follow the Supreme Court.

3. Per incuriam: Where the earlier Court of Appeal decision was given per incuriam — in ignorance of a relevant, binding statutory provision or case authority that would have materially affected the decision.

⚠️ Exam trap: A fourth exception does not exist. The Court of Appeal cannot depart from its own previous decision simply because it considers it wrong — this is the critical distinction from the Supreme Court's position under the Practice Statement 1966 (see FLK1-LSYS-038).

⚠️ Asymmetry trap: These three exceptions apply to the Court of Appeal only. The Supreme Court is not subject to Young v Bristol — it operates under the Practice Statement 1966. Do not apply the three exceptions to the SC, or the Practice Statement test to the CA.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.summary_line: YOUNG v BRISTOL AEROPLANE · THREE EXCEPTIONS · CONFLICTING CA DECISIONS · SC INCONSISTENCY · PER INCURIAM · EXHAUSTIVE LIST · ⚠️ NO FOURTH EXCEPTION · ⚠️ CA ONLY NOT SC

Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.subtopic: Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aeroplane
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.front: Does the Criminal Division of the Court of Appeal have greater flexibility than the Civil Division when departing from its own previous decisions?
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.answer:
Yes. The Criminal Division of the Court of Appeal has slightly greater flexibility than the Civil Division. While it remains subject to the three Young v Bristol Aeroplane exceptions, it may also depart from a previous decision where following it would cause injustice to a defendant — particularly where a prior decision misunderstood the law in a way that wrongly restricted the liberty of a person.

This additional flexibility reflects the higher stakes in criminal cases: an incorrect precedent in civil cases causes financial harm, but an incorrect criminal precedent may mean individuals are wrongly convicted or sentenced.

Principle articulated in: R v Taylor [1950]; R v Spencer [1985].

⚠️ Important limits: This flexibility is narrow and applies only where the liberty of defendants is at stake. The Criminal Division is not free to depart from its own decisions whenever it disagrees with them. The additional flexibility is an extension of, not a replacement for, the Young v Bristol exceptions.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.summary_line: CRIMINAL DIVISION · ADDITIONAL FLEXIBILITY · INJUSTICE TO DEFENDANT · LIBERTY AT STAKE · R v TAYLOR · R v SPENCER · NARROW EXTENSION · NOT GENERAL DISCRETION

Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.subtopic: Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aeroplane
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.front: Young v Bristol Aeroplane Co Ltd [1944] KB 718
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.issue: Whether the Court of Appeal (Civil Division) was bound by its own previous decisions, and if so whether any exceptions existed.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.rule:
The Court of Appeal is generally bound by its own previous decisions. Three exhaustive exceptions exist:

1. where there are two conflicting Court of Appeal decisions — the later court must choose which to follow
2. where a Court of Appeal decision, though not expressly overruled, cannot stand with a subsequent House of Lords (now Supreme Court) decision
3. where a previous Court of Appeal decision was given per incuriam — in ignorance of a relevant binding statute or authority.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.application: The court was asked to reconsider one of its own earlier decisions on the compensation payable to an employee injured in a wartime industrial accident. Lord Greene MR undertook a systematic survey of Court of Appeal practice and articulated the principle that the CA is bound by its own previous decisions as a matter of necessary discipline, subject only to three clearly defined exceptions. The three exceptions are exhaustive — the CA has no general discretion to depart from its own previous decisions.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.conclusion:
Young v Bristol Aeroplane remains the governing authority on Court of Appeal self-binding. The three exceptions are exhaustive for the Civil Division — there is no general discretion to depart from a previous CA decision merely because the court considers it wrong.

⚠️ The Criminal Division has slightly greater flexibility where following a previous decision would cause injustice to a defendant (R v Taylor [1950]). The SC operates under the much wider Practice Statement 1966 test.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.summary_line: CA BOUND BY OWN DECISIONS · THREE EXCEPTIONS · CONFLICTING CA DECISIONS · SC INCONSISTENCY · PER INCURIAM · EXHAUSTIVE LIST · LORD GREENE MR · NO GENERAL DISCRETION

Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.subtopic: Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aeroplane
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.front: ⚠️ TRAP SCENARIO — The Court of Appeal (Civil Division) is faced with a contract case. A barrister argues that the CA should depart from one of its own previous decisions because: (a) the CA decided the previous case without being referred to a directly relevant House of Lords authority; and (b) the CA considers the previous decision was simply wrong. Which of these grounds — if either — justifies departure?
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.answer:
Only ground (a) potentially justifies departure. Ground (b) does not.

Ground (a) — previous case decided per incuriam (without reference to a House of Lords authority): This is the third Young v Bristol Aeroplane exception. A previous CA decision given per incuriam — i.e. in ignorance of a relevant, binding statute or authority — is not binding on a later CA panel. If the CA decided the previous case without being referred to a directly relevant binding HL decision that would have materially affected the outcome, the earlier decision was per incuriam and the CA may depart from it.

Requirements for per incuriam: the HL authority must have been binding, relevant, not cited, and capable of materially changing the outcome. All must be satisfied — the bar is high and applied narrowly.

Ground (b) — the CA considers the decision was simply wrong: This is not a ground for departure. Young v Bristol Aeroplane makes clear that the three exceptions are exhaustive. The CA has no general discretion to depart from its own decisions because it considers them wrong. That is the critical difference between the CA (bound by Young) and the SC (free under the Practice Statement 1966 whenever 'it appears right to do so').

⚠️ The asymmetry trap: students apply the Practice Statement test ('appears right to do so') to the CA. They cannot. The CA's only grounds for departure are the three exhaustive Young v Bristol exceptions.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.summary_line: ⚠️ YOUNG v BRISTOL AEROPLANE · GROUND (a) = PER INCURIAM (POTENTIALLY) · GROUND (b) = NOT A GROUND · CA CANNOT DEPART MERELY BECAUSE WRONG · ⚠️ PRACTICE STATEMENT TEST DOES NOT APPLY TO CA

Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.subtopic: Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aeroplane
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.front: The Court of Appeal (Civil Division) has two of its own conflicting decisions: Thornton v Marsh [2010], which held that a particular type of exclusion clause was void for uncertainty, and Lawson v Briggs [2018], which held that the same type of clause was valid. A new CA panel must decide a case turning on the same point. What should the panel do and what is the effect on the earlier decisions?
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.answer:
This is the first Young v Bristol Aeroplane exception: where there are two conflicting CA decisions, the later CA panel must choose which to follow, and the other is treated as implicitly overruled.

Step 1 — Identify the conflict: The two decisions are directly conflicting on the same point of law. The panel cannot follow both.

Step 2 — The Young v Bristol Aeroplane rule: Where two prior CA decisions conflict, the later panel must choose which decision to follow. It is not bound to follow either one automatically. It may select the decision it considers to be correct.

Step 3 — Choosing: The panel should consider which decision is better reasoned, consistent with Supreme Court authority, and produces the more just outcome. The decision it does not follow is treated as having been implicitly overruled — it ceases to be good law.

Step 4 — Practical approach: In practice, where one decision postdates the other, the court may treat the later decision as representing the preferable position — but it is not obliged to do so. The panel retains genuine choice.

Effect: The decision the panel follows becomes the binding CA authority on this point. The other decision is implicitly overruled and should no longer be cited as good law for that proposition.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.summary_line: YOUNG v BRISTOL AEROPLANE · FIRST EXCEPTION · CONFLICTING CA DECISIONS · LATER PANEL MUST CHOOSE · NOT BOUND TO FOLLOW EITHER · UNCHOSEN DECISION IMPLICITLY OVERRULED · GENUINE CHOICE

Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.subtopic: Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aeroplane
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.front: The Court of Appeal (Criminal Division) is considering whether to depart from one of its own previous decisions in a case involving a defendant who was wrongly convicted because of an error of law in the trial judge's direction to the jury. The previous CA decision established the legal direction that the trial judge followed. Can the Criminal Division depart?
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.answer:
Yes — the Criminal Division has additional flexibility beyond the three Young v Bristol Aeroplane exceptions that applies in exactly this type of case.

General rule: Both Civil and Criminal Divisions of the CA are bound by the three Young v Bristol exceptions. The Criminal Division cannot depart from its own decisions merely because it considers them wrong.

Additional Criminal Division flexibility: However, the Criminal Division has slightly greater flexibility than the Civil Division where following a previous decision would cause injustice to a defendant — particularly where the prior decision misunderstood the law in a way that resulted in wrongful conviction or unlawful restriction on liberty: R v Taylor [1950]; R v Spencer [1985].

Applied here: The defendant was wrongly convicted as a direct result of the trial judge following the CA's own previous (incorrect) legal direction. The defendant's liberty was affected by the legal error. This is precisely the scenario the Criminal Division's additional flexibility is designed to address — an incorrect legal precedent that has led to a wrongful conviction.

The Criminal Division may depart from the previous decision on this basis. It should state why the previous decision was wrong, what the correct legal direction should have been, and allow the appeal.

⚠️ This flexibility is narrow and relates specifically to injustice to defendants and restrictions on liberty. It is not a general discretion to disagree with previous decisions.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.summary_line: CRIMINAL DIVISION CA · ADDITIONAL FLEXIBILITY · INJUSTICE TO DEFENDANT · WRONGFUL CONVICTION · R v TAYLOR · R v SPENCER · NARROW EXTENSION OF YOUNG v BRISTOL · MAY DEPART

Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.subtopic: Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aeroplane
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

The Supreme Court is hearing an appeal in a negligence case. Counsel for the appellant argues that the Court of Appeal below was wrong to follow one of its own previous decisions, because that previous CA decision was unjust and clearly incorrect. The CA had held it was bound by its own precedent unless one of the three Young v Bristol Aeroplane exceptions applied — and none did.

In the Supreme Court, a different counsel then argues: 'The Supreme Court itself is also bound by the Young v Bristol Aeroplane framework. Before the SC can depart from any of its own previous decisions, it too must demonstrate that one of the three exceptions applies. The SC cannot simply decide it was wrong and depart — it needs a recognised exception.'
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.issue: The trap has two stages and a clever surface plausibility. First: the CA was correct to apply Young v Bristol — it is bound by its own decisions subject only to the three exhaustive exceptions. This part is right. Second: the argument extends Young v Bristol to the Supreme Court, asserting the SC is equally constrained. This is the error. Students who correctly understand that the CA is tightly bound by its own precedents may naturally extend that principle upward — if the CA cannot just disagree, why can the SC?
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.rule: The Young v Bristol Aeroplane framework applies to the Court of Appeal (Civil Division). It does not apply to the Supreme Court (or its predecessor, the House of Lords). The Supreme Court operates under the Practice Statement 1966, which permits departure from a previous decision 'when it appears right to do so' — a broad, discretionary test with no fixed categories of exception. The SC is not confined to the three Young v Bristol exceptions. It may depart whenever, weighing certainty against correctness, it considers departure to be right.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.application: The specific red herring is the elegant parallel structure of the argument: if the CA needs an exception, so must the SC. The parallelism feels logical but is constitutionally wrong. The two courts operate under entirely different precedent regimes. The CA's strict self-binding reflects its intermediate position in the hierarchy — it must maintain consistency for the courts below it. The SC's Practice Statement discretion reflects its position as the final court — it can correct errors without referring to a fixed list of exceptions. The SC should assess whether to depart from its own previous decision using the Practice Statement 1966 test, not Young v Bristol.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.conclusion: YOUNG v BRISTOL = CA ONLY. SC operates under Practice Statement 1966 — broad discretion, no fixed exceptions required. The trap: extending the CA's strict self-binding framework to the Supreme Court.
Court of Appeal and Young v Bristol Aero.summary_line: YOUNG v BRISTOL = CA ONLY · SC = PRACTICE STATEMENT 1966 · BROAD DISCRETION · NO FIXED EXCEPTIONS REQUIRED · ⚠️ TRAP: YOUNG v BRISTOL APPLIED TO SC

Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.subtopic: Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocation
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.front: What are the three civil tracks and the financial thresholds for allocation?
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.answer:
Civil claims are allocated to one of three tracks under CPR Part 26:

1. Small Claims Track: Claims up to £10,000 in value (personal injury claims: up to £1,500; housing disrepair: up to £1,000 for general damages). Informal procedure; costs recovery for legal representation is very limited.

2. Fast Track: Claims between £10,000 and £25,000 (personal injury: £1,500–£25,000). Trial usually within 30 weeks; one-day trial; fixed costs regime applies.

3. Multi-Track: Claims over £25,000, and complex lower-value cases where the court considers it appropriate.

Full case management directions; expert evidence more accessible; costs management orders (CMOs).

⚠️ Financial thresholds are subject to change — verify against current CPR Part 26. Track allocation is not determined solely by value: the court also considers the nature of the claim, the likely length of trial, and the importance of the issues.
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.summary_line: SMALL CLAIMS ≤£10,000 · FAST TRACK £10,000–£25,000 · MULTI-TRACK >£25,000 · CPR PART 26 · PI LOWER LIMITS · TRACK NOT JUST BY VALUE · ⚠️ VERIFY CURRENT THRESHOLDS

Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.subtopic: Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocation
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.front: What are the three divisions of the High Court and what types of claim does each handle?
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.answer:
1. King's Bench Division (KBD): The largest division. Contract, tort, and other common law claims above the County Court threshold. Also includes specialist courts: the Administrative Court (judicial review and statutory appeals), the Commercial Court, the Technology and Construction Court (TCC), and the Admiralty Court.

2. Chancery Division: Equity-based jurisdiction. Trusts, mortgages, real property, company law, insolvency, intellectual property, and contentious probate. Includes the Intellectual Property Enterprise Court (IPEC) and various Business and Property Courts.

3. Family Division: Matrimonial proceedings, matters relating to children (parental responsibility, wardship), international child abduction, and declarations of presumed death.

⚠️ Business and Property Courts: Since 2017, many specialist courts sit within a Business and Property Courts structure, spanning both the KBD and Chancery Division. Claims are issued in the Business and Property Courts and assigned to the relevant court/division.
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.summary_line: HIGH COURT · THREE DIVISIONS · KING'S BENCH (CONTRACT/TORT/ADMIN COURT) · CHANCERY (EQUITY/COMPANY/IP) · FAMILY · BUSINESS AND PROPERTY COURTS 2017

Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.subtopic: Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocation
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.front: Mrs Chen brings a claim for breach of contract against a kitchen fitting company. Her losses are £8,500 for the faulty installation and £900 for replacement crockery broken during the works. She has also suffered significant anxiety and distress. To which track should the claim be allocated and why?
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.answer:
The claim should be allocated to the Small Claims Track.

Track allocation is governed by CPR Part 26. The three tracks are:
— Small Claims Track: up to £10,000 (subject to specific lower limits for personal injury and housing disrepair).
— Fast Track: £10,000–£25,000.
— Multi-Track: over £25,000, or complex lower-value cases.

Mrs Chen's financial losses total £8,500 + £900 = £9,400. This is below the £10,000 small claims threshold.

Distress and anxiety: damages for distress and anxiety in a consumer contract claim may be recoverable (Farley v Skinner [2001]) but the quantum is unlikely to push the total above £10,000 on these facts — loss of amenity and distress awards in consumer cases tend to be modest.

Since the total value of the claim is likely to remain below £10,000, the claim should be allocated to the Small Claims Track.

Implication: the Small Claims Track involves informal procedure and very limited costs recovery — solicitor's costs are generally not recoverable even if Mrs Chen wins. She may wish to represent herself or weigh whether engaging a solicitor is cost-effective.
Civil Court Hierarchy and Track Allocati.summary_line: SMALL CLAIMS TRACK · £10,000 THRESHOLD · TOTAL CLAIM £9,400 + DISTRESS · DISTRESS UNLIKELY TO PUSH ABOVE THRESHOLD · CPR PART 26 · LIMITED COSTS RECOVERY ON SMALL CLAIMS

Criminal Court Hierarchy.front: What is the distinction between summary, either-way, and indictable offences?
Criminal Court Hierarchy.answer:
1. Summary offences: Tried only in the Magistrates' Court before magistrates (or a District Judge). Less serious offences — e.g. most road traffic offences, common assault (s.39 CJA 1988), criminal damage up to £5,000. No right to jury trial.

2. Indictable-only offences: Tried only in the Crown Court before a judge and jury. Most serious offences — murder, manslaughter, rape, robbery, serious fraud. Magistrates conduct a preliminary 'sending' hearing and send the case to the Crown Court.

3. Either-way offences: May be tried in either the Magistrates' Court or the Crown Court.

Examples: theft, fraud, assault occasioning actual bodily harm (s.47 OAPA 1861). The mode of trial procedure applies: the defendant has the right to elect Crown Court trial (and therefore a jury).
Criminal Court Hierarchy.summary_line: SUMMARY · EITHER-WAY · INDICTABLE ONLY · MAGISTRATES' COURT · CROWN COURT · RIGHT TO ELECT JURY · MODE OF TRIAL · SENDING PROCEDURE

Criminal Court Hierarchy.front: What is the mode of trial procedure for either-way offences?
Criminal Court Hierarchy.answer:
The mode of trial procedure for either-way offences proceeds as follows:

1. Plea before venue: The defendant indicates a plea. If guilty, the Magistrates' Court proceeds to sentence or, if it considers its sentencing powers insufficient, commits to the Crown Court for sentencing.

2. Allocation hearing (if not guilty plea): The Magistrates' Court considers whether to allocate the case to the Crown Court or to retain it. It applies the Sentencing Guidelines to assess whether its sentencing powers are adequate.

3. Magistrates decline jurisdiction: If the bench decides the case is too serious, it must be sent to the Crown Court. The defendant has no say.

4. Magistrates accept jurisdiction — defendant's election: If the bench accepts jurisdiction, the defendant is given a choice — summary trial in the Magistrates' Court, or election to be tried in the Crown Court by jury.

⚠️ Once the defendant elects Crown Court trial, the case proceeds there regardless. The prosecution cannot insist on summary trial once the defendant has elected jury trial.
Criminal Court Hierarchy.summary_line: EITHER-WAY MODE OF TRIAL · PLEA BEFORE VENUE · ALLOCATION HEARING · MAGISTRATES ACCEPT/DECLINE · DEFENDANT ELECTION · RIGHT TO JURY TRIAL · CROWN COURT IF DECLINED

Criminal Court Hierarchy.front: What are the sentencing powers of the Magistrates' Court?
Criminal Court Hierarchy.answer:
The Magistrates' Court's sentencing powers are limited:

Custodial sentence: Up to 6 months for a single summary or either-way offence. The Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022 raised this to 12 months, but implementation has been delayed — check current commencement position.

For two or more either-way offences in the same proceedings: up to 12 months in aggregate (under the pre-2022 position).

Financial penalties: Up to £5,000 for most summary offences. The Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012 removed the cap for certain offences — unlimited fines available for some regulatory offences.

Other powers: Community orders, conditional and absolute discharges, compensation orders, driving disqualifications.

Committal for sentence: Where a defendant pleads guilty or is convicted of an either-way offence and the Magistrates' Court's powers prove insufficient, it may commit to the Crown Court for sentencing under s.14 Sentencing Act 2020.

⚠️ Recommend verifying current custodial limits — implementation of the 12-month provision under the JRCA 2022 has been subject to ongoing commencement decisions.
Criminal Court Hierarchy.summary_line: MAGISTRATES' SENTENCING · 6 MONTHS CUSTODY (SINGLE OFFENCE) · JRCA 2022 RAISES TO 12 MONTHS (CHECK IMPLEMENTATION) · £5,000 FINE · COMMIT TO CROWN COURT · ⚠️ VERIFY

Criminal Court Hierarchy.front: Mr Adeyemi is charged with theft of goods worth £2,000 from his employer. Theft is an either-way offence. He has indicated a not guilty plea. Advise Mr Adeyemi on: (a) how the mode of trial procedure will operate; (b) whether he has any right to insist on a Crown Court trial; and (c) what happens if the Magistrates' Court declines jurisdiction.
Criminal Court Hierarchy.answer:
(a) Mode of trial procedure:
Since Mr Adeyemi has indicated a not guilty plea, the Magistrates' Court will hold an allocation hearing. The bench will consider whether its sentencing powers are adequate if he is convicted (applying the Sentencing Guidelines). For theft of £2,000 from an employer — an aggravated type of theft — the magistrates may consider this suitable for summary trial, given their sentencing powers for either-way offences (up to 6 months' custody, or up to 12 months in aggregate —

⚠️ verify current JRCA 2022 implementation).

(b) Right to insist on Crown Court trial:
Yes — if the Magistrates' Court accepts jurisdiction, Mr Adeyemi has an absolute right to elect Crown Court trial by jury. This is a fundamental common law right for either-way offences. He may choose Crown Court trial even if the magistrates have accepted jurisdiction and even if the prosecution does not object.

(c) If the Magistrates' Court declines jurisdiction:
If the bench considers its sentencing powers insufficient (e.g. given the employment context and breach of trust), it will decline jurisdiction and send the case to the Crown Court. Mr Adeyemi has no right to insist on remaining in the Magistrates' Court — the Crown Court decision is final once the bench declines.
Criminal Court Hierarchy.summary_line: EITHER-WAY OFFENCE · ALLOCATION HEARING · DEFENDANT ELECTION TO CROWN COURT · ABSOLUTE RIGHT IF MAGISTRATES ACCEPT · MAGISTRATES CAN DECLINE → CROWN COURT MANDATORY

Criminal Court Hierarchy.front: Mr Boateng is convicted in the Magistrates' Court of two separate either-way offences: one count of fraud (6 months maximum) and one count of assault occasioning actual bodily harm (6 months maximum). The magistrates wish to sentence him to 10 months' custody in total. Can they?
Criminal Court Hierarchy.answer:
Yes — the magistrates can impose 10 months in total for two either-way offences, subject to the pre-JRCA 2022 position.

Sentencing powers:
— For a single either-way offence: the Magistrates' Court's maximum custodial sentence is 6 months.
— For two or more either-way offences sentenced in the same proceedings: the aggregate maximum is 12 months.

Mr Boateng has two either-way offences. A 10-month total custodial sentence — for example, 6 months for fraud and 4 months for ABH, consecutive — is within the magistrates' aggregate 12-month power for two either-way offences.

⚠️ JRCA 2022 caveat: The Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022 raised the single-offence limit to 12 months, which when in force would increase powers further. However, commencement of this provision has been delayed and the pre-2022 position (6 months single / 12 months aggregate) may still be in force. Verify current implementation before advising definitively.

Conclusion on these facts: 10 months is within the magistrates' current aggregate powers and is lawful, subject to the above caveat.
Criminal Court Hierarchy.summary_line: MAGISTRATES' SENTENCING · TWO EITHER-WAY OFFENCES · AGGREGATE MAX 12 MONTHS · 10 MONTHS WITHIN POWERS · SINGLE OFFENCE = 6 MONTHS · ⚠️ JRCA 2022 RAISED TO 12 MONTHS (VERIFY COMMENCEMENT)

Appellate Routes.front: What is the civil appellate route from the County Court to the Supreme Court?
Appellate Routes.summary_line: CIVIL APPELLATE ROUTE · COUNTY COURT → CA → SUPREME COURT · PERMISSION TO APPEAL · LEAPFROG: HIGH COURT → SC · AJA 1969 · GENERAL PUBLIC IMPORTANCE · ALL PARTIES CONSENT

Appellate Routes.front: What are the appellate routes from the Magistrates' Court in criminal proceedings?
Appellate Routes.answer:
Two routes from the Magistrates' Court:

1. Appeal to the Crown Court (defendant only):
— For conviction appeals: the case is reheard de novo (fresh start) before a Crown Court judge and two magistrates — on fact or law.
— For sentence appeals: de novo sentencing hearing before a Crown Court judge and two magistrates.
— As of right for conviction where the plea was not guilty; permission not generally required.
— The prosecution cannot use this route.

2. Case stated to the King's Bench Division (Administrative Court) — available to either party, law only:
— The Magistrates' Court states the facts found and the legal question for the KBD to answer.
— The KBD may affirm, reverse, or remit.
— Used where the challenge is to the legal correctness of the decision, not the facts.

Further appeals:
— From the Crown Court → Court of Appeal (Criminal Division) — leave required
— From the KBD → Court of Appeal → Supreme Court

⚠️ Prosecution routes: The prosecution cannot appeal a not-guilty verdict to the Crown Court. Prosecution routes include: case stated to KBD (law only); Attorney General's Reference under s.36 Criminal Justice Act 1988 (undue leniency — to Court of Appeal only); and the limited retrial provisions under CJA 2003 Part 10 (serious cases, new and compelling evidence).
Appellate Routes.summary_line: MAGISTRATES' APPELLATE ROUTE · CROWN COURT (DEFENDANT ONLY · DE NOVO) · CASE STATED (BOTH PARTIES · LAW ONLY · KBD) · AG REFERENCE (PROSECUTION · CA ONLY) · DOUBLE JEOPARDY

Appellate Routes.front: What are the appellate routes from the Crown Court in criminal proceedings?
Appellate Routes.answer:
From the Crown Court (defendant convicted or sentenced after trial or guilty plea):

1. Court of Appeal (Criminal Division): Leave (permission) to appeal is required. The CACD may allow an appeal against conviction where it considers the conviction 'unsafe' (Criminal Appeal Act 1968, s.2). It may also hear sentence appeals.

2. Further appeal to the Supreme Court: Requires the CACD to certify that the case raises a point of law of general public importance, and either the CACD or SC grants permission.

Criminal Cases Review Commission (CCRC): Where normal appeal routes are exhausted, a convicted person may apply to the CCRC (established by Criminal Appeal Act 1995). The CCRC may refer a conviction or sentence to the CACD where it considers there is a 'real possibility' the CACD will quash it — typically on the basis of new evidence or argument.

Prosecution routes from Crown Court:
— Attorney General's Reference under s.36 CJA 1988: Prosecution may refer an unduly lenient sentence to the CACD for review.
— CJA 2003 Part 10: Prosecution may apply to CACD to order a retrial in serious cases where new and compelling evidence emerges (limited double jeopardy exception).

⚠️ The prosecution cannot appeal an acquittal from the Crown Court as of right — double jeopardy protection applies. The CJA 2003 Part 10 exception is narrow.
Appellate Routes.summary_line: CROWN COURT APPEALS · CA CRIMINAL DIVISION (LEAVE REQUIRED) · UNSAFE CONVICTION · SC (POINT OF LAW + PERMISSION) · CCRC · AG REFERENCE (UNDUE LENIENCY) · CJA 2003 PART 10

Appellate Routes.front: Ms Larsson lost a civil claim in the County Court (£45,000 claim, Multi-Track). She appealed to the Court of Appeal, which upheld the County Court decision. She now wishes to appeal to the Supreme Court. Her barrister identifies that the case involves a short point of statutory construction that has never been considered by the Supreme Court. Advise on the steps required to reach the Supreme Court.
Appellate Routes.answer:
Two stages are required to reach the Supreme Court:

Stage 1 — Permission to appeal from the Court of Appeal:
Ms Larsson must obtain permission to appeal from the CA (Civil Division) or, if refused, directly from the Supreme Court. For a second appeal (from the CA to the SC), the SC requires the CA or SC to certify that the appeal raises a point of law of general public importance — this is a stricter threshold than a first appeal.

Stage 2 — Supreme Court grant of permission:
Even with a certified point, the Supreme Court itself must grant permission to appeal. The SC grants permission only where the case raises a point of law of general public importance.

Is the threshold met here? An unresolved point of statutory construction can qualify as a point of law of general public importance — but only where the point has broader significance beyond this case and its resolution would affect many parties or areas of law. A narrow point of statutory construction affecting only this type of dispute is less likely to attract SC interest.

Leapfrog: Ms Larsson has already been to the CA, so leapfrog (AJA 1969) is irrelevant — that procedure bypasses the CA directly from the High Court, not from the CA to the SC.

Advice: Ms Larsson should apply for permission. The prospects depend on whether the statutory construction point has sufficient broader significance to attract SC interest.
Appellate Routes.summary_line: CIVIL APPEAL ROUTE · COUNTY COURT → CA → SC · PERMISSION REQUIRED · POINT OF LAW OF GENERAL PUBLIC IMPORTANCE · LEAPFROG IRRELEVANT (ALREADY AT CA) · SC GRANTS PERMISSION

Appellate Routes.front: ⚠️ TRAP SCENARIO — Mr Khatri was charged with a summary offence and acquitted by the Magistrates' Court. The Crown Prosecution Service is dissatisfied with the outcome and wishes to appeal the acquittal. A CPS lawyer says the prosecution can appeal to the Crown Court because that is the normal appeal route from the Magistrates' Court. Is the CPS lawyer correct?
Appellate Routes.answer:
No. The CPS lawyer is wrong. The Crown Court appeal route is not available to the prosecution.

The appeal to the Crown Court from the Magistrates' Court (which involves a de novo rehearing) is available to the defendant only — for conviction appeals (fact or law) and sentence appeals. The prosecution cannot use this route.

⚠️ The trap: assuming both parties can use the Crown Court appeal route. Only defendants can appeal to the Crown Court from the Magistrates' Court.

Prosecution's available routes:

1. Case stated to the King's Bench Division (Administrative Court) — law only. The Magistrates' Court states the facts and the legal question; the KBD rules on the law. This is available to either party but is confined to a question of law — it cannot revisit findings of fact.

2. Attorney General's Reference — available only where the Attorney General considers a point of law requires clarification. This does not affect Mr Khatri's acquittal — it results in a ruling on the law for future cases, not a retrial.

3. Double jeopardy: In any event, Mr Khatri was acquitted of a summary offence.

Retrials following acquittal are only available in the very limited circumstances of CJA 2003 Part 10 — which applies only to qualifying serious offences, not summary offences.

Conclusion: The prosecution cannot appeal Mr Khatri's acquittal to the Crown Court. The case stated route (law only) is the prosecution's primary avenue if it believes the Magistrates made an error of law.
Appellate Routes.summary_line: ⚠️ CROWN COURT APPEAL = DEFENDANT ONLY · PROSECUTION CANNOT USE · PROSECUTION ROUTES: CASE STATED (LAW) · AG REFERENCE (LAW, NO RETRIAL) · DOUBLE JEOPARDY · CPS LAWYER WRONG

Appellate Routes.front: A High Court judge has decided a complex contract case raising a novel point of statutory construction of the Commercial Contracts Act 2024. Both parties consent to bypassing the Court of Appeal and appealing directly to the Supreme Court. The point of law affects the entire commercial sector. Advise on whether a leapfrog appeal is available and what conditions must be satisfied.
Appellate Routes.answer:
A leapfrog appeal may be available. The conditions under the Administration of Justice Act 1969 are:

1. The case must be in the High Court — ✓ satisfied. The leapfrog procedure applies from the High Court only, not the County Court or the Court of Appeal.

2. The point of law must be of general public importance — ✓ satisfied on these facts. A novel point of statutory construction of a major commercial statute affecting the entire commercial sector qualifies.

3. The point involves: (a) the construction of a statute or statutory instrument; or (b) a question where the CA is bound by its own previous decision or by a HL/SC decision — ✓ condition (a) is satisfied (construction of the Commercial Contracts Act 2024).

4. All parties consent — ✓ satisfied (both parties have consented).

5. The Supreme Court grants permission — the SC must independently grant permission.

Consent of the parties and satisfaction of the threshold conditions do not guarantee permission — the SC has discretion.

**Procedure:** The High Court judge grants a certificate that the conditions are met; the SC then considers whether to grant permission.

Advice: the leapfrog conditions appear to be met. The parties should apply to the High Court judge for a certificate, then petition the Supreme Court for permission. Given the commercial importance of the point, permission is plausible.
Appellate Routes.summary_line: LEAPFROG APPEAL · AJA 1969 · FROM HIGH COURT ONLY · POINT OF GENERAL PUBLIC IMPORTANCE · STATUTORY CONSTRUCTION · ALL PARTIES CONSENT · SC GRANTS PERMISSION · NOT AUTOMATIC

Appellate Routes.front: Ms Petrov was convicted of robbery (an indictable-only offence) in the Crown Court and sentenced to 7 years' imprisonment. She believes the conviction is unsafe because the judge misdirected the jury on the elements of the offence, and that the sentence is excessive. Advise on her appeal options.
Appellate Routes.answer:
Ms Petrov has two separate grounds of appeal, each with its own route.

Appeal against conviction:
Ms Petrov must apply for leave (permission) to appeal to the Court of Appeal (Criminal Division). Under the Criminal Appeal Act 1968, s.2, the CACD will allow an appeal against conviction where it considers the conviction 'unsafe'. A misdirection to the jury on the elements of the offence is a strong ground — if the jury may have convicted on an incorrect legal basis, the conviction is likely unsafe.

Appeal against sentence:
Ms Petrov may also seek leave to appeal against sentence to the CACD. The sentence must be 'wrong in principle' or 'manifestly excessive' — 7 years for robbery may or may not be excessive depending on the facts; the CACD will assess this against the Sentencing Guidelines.

Further appeal to Supreme Court:
If the CACD refuses or upholds the conviction/sentence, Ms Petrov may apply to the SC if: (a) the CACD certifies a point of law of general public importance; and (b) either the CACD or SC grants permission.

Criminal Cases Review Commission:
